content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 13, 1994

Joe Pandit
6929 Azalea Drive
San Jose, CA 95120

Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. I-94-152

Dear Mr. Pandit:

You have requested advice regarding potential conflicts
arising out of your intended candidacy for two elective offices.
The Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000-91015,
does not address this issue. As we cannot advise you on matters
outside our jurisdiction, we are unable to answer the questions
you have raised.

The Attorney General's Office has issued many opinions on the
incompatibility of simultaneously held offices. In particular, we
direct your attention to 65 Ops. Att'y. Gen. 606 (1982) which
addresses the compatibility of the office of city councilmember
and school trustee. California law may prohibit an individual
from running for two incompatible offices simultaneously. See
Keane v. Smith, 4 Cal.3d 932, 942-43, 95 Cal. Rptr. 197 (1971).
Both the opinions of the attorney general and the Keene opinion
are available at your county law library.

Any further questions you may have on this particular matter
are more properly addressed to either: the office of the attorney
general; the office of the county counsel; the office of the city
attorney; or the legal counsel for the college district for which
you intend to run.

We regret that we are unable to answer your questions. In
the future, should you have questions concerning your obligations
and responsibilities under the Political Reform Act, this office
remains available to assist you.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General-Counsel

By Daniel E. Muallem
Counsel, Legal Division

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

sections.question: Your Request For Advice regarding potential conflicts arising out of your intended candidacy for two elective offices.
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000—91015, does not address this issue. As we cannot advise you on matters outside of our jurisdiction, we are unable to answer the questions you have raised.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the Political Reform Act address conflicts of interest arising from candidacy for two elective offices simultaneously?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC lacks jurisdiction over the issue of simultaneous candidacy for multiple offices, as this matter is outside the scope of the Political Reform Act. The inquirer should consult the Attorney General's Office, county counsel, city attorney, or college district legal counsel for guidance on office compatibility.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Declination letter with no formal Q/C sections; however, the request and conclusion are clearly identifiable. The letter references relevant Attorney General opinions and case law rather than providing FPPC guidance.

QUESTION: Your Request For Advice regarding potential conflicts arising out of your intended candidacy for two elective offices.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000—91015, does not address this issue. As we cannot advise you on matters outside of our jurisdiction, we are unable to answer the questions you have raised.

The Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 81000-91015, does not address this issue. The Attorney General's Office has issued many opinions on the incompatibility of simultaneously held offices. In particular, we direct your attention to 65 Ops. Att'y. Gen. 606 (1982) which addresses the compatibility of the office of city councilmember and school trustee. California law may prohibit an individual from running for two incompatible offices simultaneously. See Keane v. Smith, 4 Cal.3d 932, 942-43, 95 Cal. Rptr. 197 (1971). Both the opinions of the attorney general and the Keene opinion are available at your county law library. Any further questions you may have on this particular matter are more properly addressed to either: the office of the attorney general; the office of the county counsel; the office of the city attorney; or the legal counsel for the college district for which you intend to run. We regret that we are unable to answer your questions. Churchwell General-Counsel By Daniel E. Muallem Counsel, Legal Division 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660
embedding.summary: FPPC declines to provide advice on potential conflicts arising from intended candidacy for two elective offices, determining the matter falls outside the Political Reform Act's jurisdiction. The letter directs the inquirer to the Attorney General's Office and other appropriate legal authorities.
